slides.title: Tuples: Sealed Boxes of Data
slides.body: A **tuple** is an ordered collection of values — like a list, but **locked**. Once you create it, the contents can never change.
slides.tip: Immutability is a feature: it guarantees the data stays exactly as you left it.

slides.title: Creating a Tuple
slides.body: Wrap values in **parentheses** `()` separated by commas. The parentheses are optional — the commas are what make it a tuple.
slides.tip: Single-element tuple needs a trailing comma: `(42,)` — otherwise `(42)` is just the number 42.

slides.title: Access & Unpack Elements
slides.body: Index a tuple with `[]` just like a list. Even better, **unpack** all values into separate variables in one clean line.
slides.code:
color = (255, 128, 0)
r, g, b = color
print(r, g, b)
slides.tip: Unpacking is one of Python's most satisfying tricks — one line, zero mess.

slides.title: Tuples Are Immutable
slides.body: Try to change a value inside a tuple and Python raises a `TypeError`. This isn't a bug — it's the whole point.
slides.output: TypeError: 'tuple' object does not support item assignment
slides.tip: Use a list when data must change; use a tuple when data must stay fixed.

slides.title: Return Multiple Values Easily
slides.body: Functions can only `return` one thing — make that one thing a **tuple** to send back several results at once.
slides.tip: Tuple unpacking at the call site keeps the code clean and readable.

slides.title: You've Got Tuples!
slides.body: Tuples are **ordered**, **immutable**, and **unpackable**. Reach for them when data is fixed — coordinates, colors, dates, or multiple return values.
slides.tip: Bonus superpower: tuples can be dictionary keys; lists cannot — because tuples are hashable.
